slides.title: Share Your Views
bullets: Prepare to read, discuss and write
slides.narration: Welcome to Grade 5 English, Unit 8: Share Your Views. Welcome to Unit 8! In this unit you will learn how people share their views and try to persuade others. Persuasive writing and speaking are everywhere: in posters that ask you to join an event, in letters to the headteacher asking for something new, in film reviews that recommend a movie, and even in the conversations you have with your friends every day. By the end of this unit, you will know how to tell the difference between a fact and an opinion, how to use powerful language to persuade an audience, and how to structure a piece of writing that makes people sit up and listen. In this short lecture, preview the key ideas and prepare for the independent lesson.

slides.title: What you will achieve
bullets: Recognise and write persuasive texts such as posters, letters, and reviews
Tell the difference between a fact (something that can be proved) and an opinion (what someone thinks or feels)
Use comparative and superlative adjectives to make your arguments stronger
slides.narration: By the end of this unit, you will be able to recognise and write persuasive texts such as posters, letters, and reviews. you will also tell the difference between a fact (something that can be proved) and an opinion (what someone thinks or feels). you will also use comparative and superlative adjectives to make your arguments stronger. Keep these goals in mind as you work.

slides.title: Language for precise thinking
slides.narration: Your vocabulary work begins with these words: intelligence, citizenship, physician, geographer, ancient, magnificent. Listen to each pronunciation in the vocabulary lab, study its meaning in context, and use the word accurately in your own discussion and writing.

slides.title: Read critically
slides.narration: The reading sequence includes Share Your Views: source text 1 and Share Your Views: source text 2. Read actively. Identify each text's central ideas, notice viewpoint and reliability, and support every inference with relevant evidence from the text.

slides.title: The Present Perfect Tense
bullets: What it means and why it matters The present perfect tense is a special way to talk about actions that happened in the past but are still important now....
slides.narration: The first language focus is The Present Perfect Tense. What it means and why it matters The present perfect tense is a special way to talk about actions that happened in the past but are still important now. It connects the past with the present. We use it to talk about experiences, achievements, and situations that continue. For example, "She has studied philosophy and physics" means she studied those subjects before, and that knowledge is still relevant today. Notice the structure in the model examples, then apply it deliberately in connected sentences and paragraphs.

slides.title: Use English for a real purpose
bullets: Explain, support and improve your ideas
slides.narration: In speaking, you will complete Speaking 1: Present Your Views. Organise your ideas, use evidence and respond clearly to questions. In writing, you will plan, draft, check and revise. Use the success criteria before submitting your work.

slides.title: You are ready to begin
slides.narration: You are ready to begin. Start with the vocabulary lab, continue through reading and comprehension, apply the grammar focus, and complete the speaking and writing practices. Use AI English for hints and feedback, and bring your questions to the live sessions.
